q: An object is placed at the centre of curvature of a concave mirror. The image is formed:
sol: When an object is at C of a concave mirror, the image is real, inverted, and formed at C.

q: The refractive index of transparent medium A is 1.5 and medium B is 1.33. In which medium does light travel faster?
sol: Speed of light is inversely proportional to the refractive index (v = c/n).

q: Which of the following can make a parallel beam of light when light from a point source is incident on it?
o: Both concave mirror and convex lens
sol: Light from a point source at the focus of a concave mirror or convex lens emerges as parallel rays.

q: A convex lens has a focal length of 20 cm. Its power is:
The mirror used by dentists to see large images of teeth is:
sol: Concave mirrors produce an enlarged virtual image when the object is between P and F.

q: The SI unit of power of a lens is:
sol: Dioptre (D) is the unit, defined as the power of a lens with 1m focal length.

q: A ray of light traveling from a denser medium to a rarer medium bends:
sol: In a rarer medium, the speed of light increases, causing it to bend away from the normal.

q: The focal length of a plane mirror is:
sol: A plane mirror has no curvature, so its focal length is considered infinite.

q: An object is 10 cm in front of a plane mirror. The distance between the object and its image is:
sol: Image distance equals object distance (10cm + 10cm = 20cm).

q: Magnification produced by a rear-view mirror fitted in vehicles:
o: Can be more or less than 1
sol: Rear-view mirrors are convex and always produce diminished images.

q: Which lens is also known as a diverging lens?
sol: A concave lens diverges parallel rays of light incident on it.

q: According to the Cartesian sign convention, the focal length of a convex mirror is:
sol: The focus of a convex mirror is behind the mirror, in the direction of incident light.

q: If the magnification produced by a lens is -2, the image is:
sol: A negative sign in magnification always indicates a real and inverted image.

q: The speed of light in vacuum is approximately:
sol: The universal constant c is 3 x 10^8 m/s.

q: A spherical mirror whose reflecting surface is curved inwards is called:
sol: Inward curvature defines a concave mirror.

q: The relationship between radius of curvature (R) and focal length (f) is:
sol: For spherical mirrors of small aperture, the focal length is half the radius of curvature.

q: No matter how far you stand from a mirror, your image appears erect. The mirror is likely:
sol: Both plane and convex mirrors always form erect images.

q: When light passes from air to glass, the quantity that does not change is:
sol: Frequency is a characteristic of the source and remains constant during refraction.

q: The power of a concave lens of focal length 2m is:
sol: P = 1/f. Since f is negative for concave (-2m), P = -0.5 D.

q: The point on the principal axis where all parallel rays meet after reflection is:
sol: This is the definition of the principal focus.

q: A ray of light strikes a plane mirror at an angle of incidence of 30 degrees. The angle of reflection is:
sol: Law of Reflection: Angle of incidence = Angle of reflection.

q: For a real object, which of the following can form a real image?
sol: Only concave mirrors (and convex lenses) can form real images.

q: The refractive index of water is 1.33. What does this mean?
o: Speed of light in water is 1.33 times c
sol: Refractive index is the ratio of speed of light in vacuum to speed in medium.

q: To get a magnification of -1 with a convex lens, the object should be placed:
sol: At 2F, the image is real, inverted, and same size as the object (m = -1).

q: A virtual image larger than the object can be produced by:
sol: Concave mirrors produce enlarged virtual images when the object is very close (P-F).

q: The unit of refractive index is:
sol: Refractive index is a ratio of similar quantities, hence dimensionless.

q: In which of the following is the speed of light maximum?
sol: Light travels fastest in a vacuum where there is no medium to obstruct it.

q: Which of the following is the lens formula?
sol: 1/v - 1/u = 1/f is the standard lens formula.

q: A ray of light incident on a glass slab normally (at 90 degrees to surface) undergoes:
sol: Light incident along the normal does not bend; angle of refraction is 0.

q: The effective diameter of the circular outline of a spherical mirror is:
sol: This is the definition of the aperture of a mirror.

q: A convex lens forms a real, inverted image of an needle at 50 cm from it. Where is the needle if image size = object size?
sol: m=-1 means v=2f. If v=50cm, then u must be 50cm on the other side.

q: Snell's law is expressed as:
sol: The ratio of sine of angle of incidence to sine of angle of refraction is constant.

q: The refractive index of diamond is 2.42. The speed of light in diamond is:
sol: Higher refractive index means lower speed of light.

q: Where should an object be placed in front of a convex lens to get a real image of the size of the object?
sol: Placing at 2F (twice focal length) gives a same-sized real image.

q: An object is placed 20 cm from a concave mirror of focal length 10 cm. The image is:
sol: Object is at 2F (C), so image is real and inverted.

q: Which of the following is the mirror formula?
sol: 1/v + 1/u = 1/f is the standard mirror formula.

q: The power of a combination of two lenses of power +2D and -1D is:
If the image is always virtual, erect and diminished, the mirror is:
sol: This is the characteristic of image formation by a convex mirror.

q: Light travels in:
sol: Rectilinear propagation is the property of light traveling in straight lines.

q: A full-length image of a distant tall building can definitely be seen by using:
sol: Convex mirrors have a wider field of view and always form diminished images.

q: Refractive index of glass with respect to air is 3/2. Refractive index of air with respect to glass is:
sol: n21 = 1/n12. Reciprocal of 1.5 is 0.66 (2/3).

q: The center of the reflecting surface of a spherical mirror is:
sol: The pole (P) is the geometric center of the mirror's surface.

q: When an object is placed between F and P of a concave mirror, the image is:
sol: The only case where a concave mirror forms a virtual image is when the object is between P and F.

q: Magnification (m) for a mirror is:
sol: For mirrors, m = -v/u. For lenses, m = v/u.

q: The focal length of a lens of power -4.0 D is:
Which of the following is used in car headlights?
sol: Concave mirrors are used to get a powerful parallel beam of light.

q: If incident ray passes through focus of a concave mirror, reflected ray will:
sol: Principal of reversibility: rays through F become parallel to the axis.

q: An object at infinity of a concave mirror forms an image at:
sol: Parallel rays from infinity converge at the focus.

q: The sign of magnification for a virtual image is always:
sol: Virtual images are erect, so height of image is positive, making m positive.

q: The ratio of speed of light in air to speed of light in medium is:
sol: n = c/v is the absolute refractive index.

q: A ray of light is incident on a convex lens parallel to principal axis. It will:
sol: Parallel rays converge at the focus F2 of a convex lens.

q: Optical center of a lens is denoted by:
sol: O is the central point of a lens.

q: The image formed by a concave lens is always:
sol: Concave lenses always form virtual, erect, and diminished images.

q: If the radius of curvature of a mirror is 32 cm, its focal length is:
A ray passing through the optical center of a lens will:
sol: Light through the optical center O does not suffer any deviation.

q: If an image is real and inverted, magnification m will be:
sol: Real/inverted images have a negative height relative to the object.

q: To get a virtual image with a convex lens, the object distance (u) must be:
sol: Virtual images form when the object is between O and F.

q: The change in direction of light when it passes from one medium to another is:
sol: Refraction is the bending of light due to change in speed.

q: Which of the following materials cannot be used to make a lens?
sol: Clay is opaque and light cannot pass through it.

q: A diverging mirror is:
sol: Convex mirrors diverge light rays.

q: Total power of three lenses with powers 1D, 2D and 3D is:
The image distance v for a virtual image in a mirror is:
sol: Virtual images are behind the mirror, hence positive by convention.

q: Image formed by a plane mirror is:
sol: Plane mirrors always form virtual, erect images of the same size.

q: If the object is at 2F1 of a convex lens, the image is at:
sol: At 2F, image is formed at 2F on the other side.

q: A lens of power +2.0 D is a:
sol: Positive power implies a converging (convex) lens.

q: The distance between the pole and principal focus of a mirror is:
sol: This is the definition of focal length (f).

q: For which position of object does a concave mirror form a virtual and erect image?
sol: This is the 'magnifying glass' position for a concave mirror.

q: A ray of light incident along the principal axis of a mirror reflects:
sol: Rays along the axis are normal to the surface (i=0, r=0).

q: Laws of reflection are valid for:
sol: Reflection laws apply to all surfaces, regardless of shape.

q: The lateral displacement of light through a glass slab depends on:
sol: Shift increases with thickness, index, and incident angle.

q: Which lens has a positive focal length?
sol: By convention, converging (convex) lenses have positive f.

q: The image formed by a convex mirror is always:
sol: Convex mirrors always produce smaller images.

q: A ray of light is refracted when it passes from glass to water. It bends:
sol: Water (1.33) is rarer than glass (1.5), so it bends away from normal.

q: Maximum refractive index is of:
sol: Diamond has one of the highest known refractive indices (~2.42).

q: If v is positive for a lens, the image is:
o: On the same side as object
sol: Positive v in lenses means the image is on the opposite side (real).

q: Power of a lens is reciprocal of its:
sol: P = 1/f (f must be in meters).

q: When object distance u = infinity for a convex lens, magnification is:
sol: Image is a point at focus, so magnification is effectively zero.

q: A ray of light is incident at an angle of 0 degrees to the normal. The angle of refraction is:
sol: Normal incidence results in no refraction/bending.

q: A concave mirror produces a magnification of +3. Where is the object?
sol: Positive m means virtual/enlarged, which happens between P and F.

q: Size of image formed by a convex mirror depends on:
sol: Image size is determined by magnification m = -v/u.

q: Which phenomenon is responsible for the twinkling of stars?
sol: Light from stars bends continuously due to changing air density.

q: The focal length of a concave mirror is 15 cm. To get an erect image, u should be:
sol: u must be less than f (15cm) to get an erect virtual image.

q: Optical density is same as mass density.
sol: Optical density relates to speed of light; a denser medium like oil can have lower mass density than water.

q: Magnification for a plane mirror is always:
sol: Image is same size and erect, so m = +1.

q: For a lens, if m = +0.5, the lens is:
sol: Diminished (+0.5) and virtual (+) images are the hallmark of concave lenses.

q: A searchlight uses which mirror?
sol: Concave mirrors provide a parallel beam of light.

q: Where should an object be placed so that a real and inverted image of very large size is formed by a convex lens?
sol: At focus, image is formed at infinity and is highly enlarged.

q: The refractive index of medium 2 with respect to medium 1 is n21 = :
sol: n21 = speed in medium 1 / speed in medium 2.

q: If the magnification is -0.5, the image is:
sol: Negative means real/inverted; 0.5 means diminished.

q: The speed of light in glass (n=1.5) is:
Parallel rays incident on a concave lens appear to diverge from:
sol: Diverging rays, when produced backwards, meet at the focus.

q: A virtual, erect and same sized image is formed by:
sol: This is the standard image property of a plane mirror.

q: The power of a lens is -2.0 D. Its focal length is:
A ray of light strikes a glass slab at an angle of 40 degrees with the surface. The angle of incidence is:
sol: Angle of incidence is measured from the normal: 90 - 40 = 50.

q: What is the nature of image formed by a convex mirror when object is at infinity?
sol: Convex mirrors always form virtual images; at infinity, it's at the focus.

q: Refraction occurs because:
o: Speed of light changes in different media
sol: Variation in speed causes the wave to change direction.

q: Which of the following is not a use of a concave mirror?
sol: Rear view mirrors must be convex to provide a wider view.

q: A spherical mirror and a thin spherical lens have each a focal length of -15 cm. The mirror and lens are likely to be:
sol: Focal length is negative for both concave mirrors and concave lenses.

q: The image of an object formed by a lens is real and inverted. If u = 20 cm and v = 20 cm, the focal length is:
sol: v=u=2f (since image is same size). 2f = 20, so f = 10 cm.

q: Magnification (m) is defined as:
sol: m = height of image (h') / height of object (h).
